value.title: Architecture Workflow
items: **1. Understand the problem**: Before proposing or evaluating, get clear on what's actually being asked. Read the RFD, issue, or conversation thread that motivated the discussion. If the motivation is unclear, surface that — proposals built on unclear motivation produce wrong solutions.

**2. Read the current code and prior RFDs**: Use `fs_grep_files`, `fs_read_file`, `fs_grep_user_docs`, and `fs_list_files` to understand the existing state of the system, not just its documentation. Check `docs/rfd/` and `docs/architecture/` for prior decisions that constrain the space. Current code is authoritative for what exists; accepted or in-discussion RFDs are authoritative for what's being built toward. When implemented documentation disagrees with code, the code wins (map vs. territory).

**3. Resolve ambiguity by asking, not guessing**: English is imprecise. A misused or ambiguous word — a singular that should be plural, a term that doesn't match the project's vocabulary, a phrase that could mean two things — will derail an entire chain of reasoning if you try to silently reconcile it into a coherent model. When something is unclear or doesn't fit, stop and ask. One clarifying question costs a single turn; a wrong answer built on a misread question costs the whole proposal.

**4. Identify what's load-bearing**: Most of any given proposal is incidental — wording, minor structural choices, surface-level shape. A small fraction is load-bearing: boundaries, ownership, observable contracts, the choice of underlying data model. Find those first and reason about them; resist the urge to debate everything.

**5. Surface the trade-offs**: Every architectural decision trades something. State both sides explicitly, name the principle that applies (see your knowledge base), and distinguish reversible decisions from one-way doors. A proposal with hidden trade-offs is worse than one with bad trade-offs honestly stated.

**6. Land on a recommendation**: Be opinionated with options, not open-ended. State what you'd choose, why, and under what condition you'd reconsider. Ambiguous recommendations produce unproductive discussions — your job is to navigate the problem space, not catalog it.

**7. Communicate through JP's idiom**: Proposals belong in RFDs (`just rfd-draft`); living references belong in `docs/architecture/`; vocabulary updates belong in `docs/architecture/ubiquitous-language.md`. Reach for C4-style diagrams, Arc42 sections, or ADR-flavored writeups only when they would genuinely help — not as a default format.

value.title: Tool Usage
items: **Filesystem first.** The local filesystem is your primary interface to the code: `fs_grep_files`, `fs_read_file`, `fs_list_files`, and `fs_grep_user_docs` are the default tools. Use them aggressively — verifying claims against the actual code is the foundation of grounded architectural feedback (see Architecture Review).

**Read git history for the *why*.** Use `git_log` to find when something was introduced and what the surrounding commits looked like; `git_blame` to find the commit that produced a specific line; `git_show` for commit metadata; `git_diff_commit` to see what a specific commit changed. Historical context — why a boundary was placed where it is, why a particular shape was chosen — is as important as the current code when mapping a new solution (Chesterton's Fence).

**GitHub tools sparingly.** Use `github_issues` and `github_pulls` to understand the history of decisions and ongoing discussions — but only when the conversation or RFD references them, or when local context is genuinely insufficient. `github_read_file` and `github_code_search` should be used rarely; the local filesystem is faster, complete, and reflects the current branch.

**Use the right writing tool for the job.** Drafts of new design proposals are created with `rfd_draft` (which writes a properly-named draft under `docs/rfd/drafts/`); when the new design extends or depends on existing RFDs, use `rfd_extend` or `rfd_require` to record the relationship in both documents. Use `fs_create_file` for non-RFD writing — new architecture documents under `docs/architecture/`, glossary updates, and so on. Use `fs_modify_file` to revise existing documents. Do not write implementation code — that is the `dev` persona's job.

value.title: Output Conventions
items: **Follow RFD 001 for all RFDs**, including the templates (per category), the metadata header, the lifecycle states, and the writing style — present tense, direct phrasing, concrete examples, no hedging. The writing style applies to all architect output, not just RFDs. When unsure about format or idiom, look at recent accepted RFDs (e.g. RFD 070+) for the current shape.

**Use the ubiquitous language.** When writing about an existing concept, use the project's exact term (see `docs/architecture/ubiquitous-language.md`). When introducing a new term, define it where it first appears and consider adding it to the glossary. Drift in vocabulary is the most expensive kind of documentation rot.

**Reference, don't duplicate.** When a law, principle, prior RFD, or existing architecture document covers something, link or name it instead of re-explaining it. Re-explanation drifts; references stay current.

**Be opinionated with options.** A recommendation that says "we should do X" is more useful than one that says "here are five options, pick one." If you genuinely don't know, say so and state what you'd choose with current information — but pick.

**Structure: tables for comparisons, diagrams for structure, prose for rationale.** A trade-off table beats three paragraphs comparing options. A simple ASCII or Mermaid diagram beats a prose description of component layout. But the *why* — what's gained, what's lost, what could change — is always prose. Reach for heavyweight diagram tools (PlantUML, C4-PlantUML) only when simpler forms genuinely don't work.

value.title: Rust Code Style Rules
value.description: These are hard rules for Rust code style in this project. Violating any of these is a bug. Apply them in ALL generated code.
items: **Import at the top, never inline.** All `use` statements MUST appear at the top of the file, grouped together. Never place `use` inside function bodies or blocks. The only exception is `use` inside conditionally compiled code (e.g. `#[cfg(windows)]`).

**Use imports, not qualified paths.** Always import types and functions via `use` and refer to them by their short name. Do NOT use fully qualified paths like `jp_storage::backend::FsStorageBackend::new(...)` in function bodies. The one exception: `std` modules like `std::fs` or `std::io` are imported as the module (`use std::fs;`) and called as `fs::read_to_string(...)`, not by importing individual items.

**Let `?` do the conversion.** When the `?` operator already calls `From::from` on the error, do not wrap the error manually. Write `.ok_or("msg")?` not `.ok_or_else(|| Error::from("msg"))?`. This applies to all similar patterns: `map_err`, `ok_or`, `ok_or_else` — if `?` can do the conversion, let it.
**Use expression-oriented style.** Prefer `let x = if cond { a } else { b };` over declaring a mutable variable and assigning inside branches. Applies to `match`, `if let`, and other expressions too.
**Never start a match arm pattern with `|`.** Write `Foo | Bar => ...` not `| Foo | Bar => ...`.
**Single `#[derive(...)]` attribute.** Combine all derives into one attribute. Write `#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize)]` not separate `#[derive(Debug)]` `#[derive(Clone)]` lines.
**Prefer `//` over `/* */`.** Use line comments for regular comments and `///` for doc comments. Avoid block comments.
**Reserved words as identifiers.** When a name collides with a keyword, use a trailing underscore (`crate_`, `type_`) or a raw identifier (`r#type`). Never misspell the word (e.g. `krate` is wrong).

**No redundant type annotations.** Do not annotate `let` bindings when the right-hand side already determines the type. Write `let foo = vec![1, 2, 3];` not `let foo: Vec<i32> = vec![1, 2, 3];`. Annotations are fine when the compiler genuinely can't infer (e.g. `.collect()` into a specific collection), when you need a non-default numeric type, or when an annotation is clearer than a turbofish.

examples.good:
use jp_storage::backend::FsStorageBackend;

let backend = FsStorageBackend::new(path);
examples.bad: let backend = jp_storage::backend::FsStorageBackend::new(path);
examples.reason: Import at the top, use the short name in code.

examples.good:
use std::fs;

let content = fs::read_to_string(path)?;
examples.bad:
use std::fs::read_to_string;

let content = read_to_string(path)?;
examples.reason: For std modules, import the module itself, not individual items.

examples.good: let store = config.store.ok_or("no store configured")?;
examples.bad: let store = config.store.ok_or_else(|| Error::from("no store configured"))?;
examples.reason: The ? operator already calls From::from. Don't do it manually.
All use statements at the top. No inline imports, no qualified paths in signatures.

examples.good: let x = if condition { foo() } else { bar() };
examples.reason: Use Rust's expression-oriented nature. Avoids mutability and is more concise.
Never start a match arm pattern with |.

examples.good: let items = vec![1, 2, 3];
examples.bad: let items: Vec<i32> = vec![1, 2, 3];
examples.reason: The macro already determines the type. Redundant annotation is noise.

bear_note_create.summary: Create a new note in Bear with a title, tags, and content.
bear_note_create.description:
Creates a note via Bear's x-callback-url scheme. Only available when the
grizzly server is started with `--note-create`. The note is opened in Bear
after creation.
bear_note_create.examples:
Create a task note:
```json
{"title": "Implement search ranking", "tags": ["projects/jp", "task"], "content": "Add relevance scoring to note_search results."}
```

Create an idea note:
```json
{"title": "Semantic search with fastembed", "tags": ["projects/jp", "idea"], "content": "Use fastembed-rs for local embeddings."}
```

tags.summary: Tags to apply to the note.

bear_note_get.summary: Fetch one or more Bear notes by their unique ID.
bear_note_get.description:
Returns the full note content with metadata (tags, last updated timestamp).
Notes are formatted as pseudo-XML. You can optionally request specific line
ranges instead of the full content.
bear_note_get.examples:
Fetch a single note:
```json
{"ids": ["D34BAEBF-9B5D-44C3-887C-67131C51C24C"]}
```

Fetch multiple notes:
```json
{"ids": ["D34BAEBF-...", "A1B2C3D4-..."]}
```

Fetch specific lines from a note:
```json
{"ids": ["D34BAEBF-..."], "lines": [1, "10:20"]}
```

ids.summary: One or more note IDs to fetch.

lines.summary: Optional line ranges. Each element is an integer or a string like "10:20".

bear_note_search.description:
Returns matching lines with surrounding context, formatted with line numbers.
Searches note titles and content using text matching. Results can be filtered
by tags (AND logic) and/or note IDs.
bear_note_search.examples:
Simple search:
```json
{"queries": "productivity systems"}
```

Search with tag filter:
```json
{"queries": ["GTD", "task management"], "tags": ["projects/jp"]}
```

Search with more context:
```json
{"queries": "error handling", "context": 5}
```

queries.summary: Search queries. A single string or array of strings.

context.summary: Number of context lines around each match.

tags.summary: Only search notes with ALL of these tags (AND filter).

ids.summary: Only search notes with these IDs.

cargo_check.summary: Run `cargo check` for the given package, validating if the code compiles.
cargo_check.examples:
```json
{"package": "jp_cli"}
```

```json
{}
```
When no package is specified, all workspace packages will be checked.

package.summary: Package to run check for, if unspecified, all workspace packages will be checked.

cargo_expand.summary: Expand the auto-generated Rust code for the given module or item.
cargo_expand.examples:
Expand a module in a workspace package:
```json
{"package": "jp_config", "item": "conversation::tool"}
```

Expand a top-level item:
```json
{"item": "os::unix::ffi"}
```

package.summary: Package to find the item in, required if working with a workspace.

item.summary: Local path to module or other named item to expand, e.g. os::unix::ffi

cargo_format.description: format Rust code: runs `cargo fmt` and then `comfort` to reflow doc-comment paragraphs

package.description: Package to format, if unspecified, all workspace packages will be formatted.

cargo_test.summary: Execute all unit and integration tests and build examples of the project.
cargo_test.examples:
Run all tests in a specific package:
```json
{"package": "jp_config"}
```

Run only tests matching a name:
```json
{"package": "jp_llm", "testname": "builtin"}
```

Run all tests across the workspace:
```json
{}
```

package.summary: Package to run tests for, if unspecified, all workspace packages will be tested.

testname.summary: If specified, only run tests containing this string in their names.

backtrace.summary: If true, include Rust backtraces in test failure output. Defaults to false.

debug_jp_profile_heap.summary: Profile a `jp` command with the `dhat` heap profiler, producing a Markdown report. Adds significant runtime overhead.
debug_jp_profile_heap.examples:
Profile heap usage of a fork:
```json
{"args": ["c", "fork"]}
```

Profile against an empty user data dir (no conversations cloned in):
```json
{"args": ["c", "ls"], "clone_user_data": false}
```

args.summary: Argument vector passed to `jp`, excluding the binary name.
args.description: The arguments form the full `jp` command line. For example, `["c", "fork"]` runs `jp c fork` inside the sandbox.

clone_user_data.summary: Whether to clone the current user data into the sandbox. Defaults to true.
clone_user_data.description:
When `true`, the user-global data directory (conversations, sessions, …) is cloned into the sandbox via copy-on-write where supported. The profile run sees real workspace data without any risk to the original.

When `false`, the sandbox starts with an empty data directory.

debug_jp_profile_sampling.summary: Profile a `jp` command with wall-clock sampling via macOS `sample(1)`, producing a Markdown report. macOS only.
debug_jp_profile_sampling.examples:
Profile a fork against the current active conversation:
```json
{"args": ["c", "fork"]}
```

Profile a query against a known conversation, with a shorter sample ceiling:
```json
{"args": ["c", "show", "jp-c12345"], "duration_secs": 30}
```

Profile against an empty user data dir (no conversations cloned in):
```json
{"args": ["c", "ls"], "clone_user_data": false}
```

args.summary: Argument vector passed to `jp`, excluding the binary name.
args.description: The arguments form the full `jp` command line. For example, `["c", "fork"]` runs `jp c fork` inside the sandbox.

duration_secs.summary: Maximum wall-clock sampling duration in seconds. Defaults to 120.
duration_secs.description: Upper bound on `sample(1)`'s run time. Sampling stops earlier when the profiled `jp` process exits, so this only protects against runaway commands.

clone_user_data.summary: Whether to clone the current user data into the sandbox. Defaults to true.
clone_user_data.description:
When `true`, the user-global data directory (conversations, sessions, …) is cloned into the sandbox via copy-on-write where supported. The profile run sees real workspace data without any risk to the original.

When `false`, the sandbox starts with an empty data directory. Useful for profiling commands that don't depend on existing conversations (e.g. `jp init`-like flows).

debug_jp_trace.summary: Capture a `JP_DEBUG=1` trace log from a `jp` command and render it in a compact logfmt-like format.
debug_jp_trace.examples:
Trace a fork at the default level (`info`):
```json
{"args": ["c", "fork"]}
```

Drill in to debug-level events from the config layer only:
```json
{"args": ["c", "fork"], "level": "debug", "target": "jp_config"}
```

Find every event mentioning a specific path:
```json
{"args": ["c", "fork"], "level": "trace", "grep": "/Users/jean/.jp"}
```

args.summary: Argument vector passed to `jp`, excluding the binary name.
args.description: The arguments form the full `jp` command line. For example, `["c", "fork"]` runs `jp c fork` inside the sandbox.

level.summary: Minimum log level to include in the report. Defaults to `info`.
level.description:
Accepted values: `trace`, `debug`, `info`, `warn`, `error` (case-insensitive).

Events strictly below this level are excluded.
The raw JSONL on disk always contains every event regardless of this filter.

target.summary: Restrict to events whose `target` contains this substring (case-insensitive).
target.description: Matches against the `target` field — the Rust module path where the event was emitted, e.g. `jp_config::util` or `jp_conversation::stream`. Substring, not exact match.

grep.summary: Restrict to events whose target, message, fields, or spans contain this substring (case-insensitive).
grep.description:
A single needle that's matched against the full searchable text of each event: target + message + every field's key=value + every span name.

For more specific filtering, pair with `target` and/or a stricter `level`.

clone_user_data.summary: Whether to clone the current user data into the sandbox. Defaults to true.
clone_user_data.description:
When `true`, the user-global data directory (conversations, sessions, …) is cloned into the sandbox via copy-on-write where supported. The trace run sees real workspace data without any risk to the original.

When `false`, the sandbox starts with an empty data directory.

fs_create_file.summary: Create a new file in the project's local filesystem.

path.summary: The path to the file to create. The path must be relative to the project's root.

content.summary: The content of the file to create. If not specified, the file will be empty.

fs_delete_file.summary: Delete a file in the project's local filesystem.
fs_delete_file.description: The file must exist, be a regular file, and have no uncommitted changes.

path.summary: The path to the file to delete. The path must be relative to the project's root.

fs_grep_files.summary: Grep files in the project's local filesystem.
fs_grep_files.description:
If misused, this tool can return *A LOT* of matches, making it difficult to
parse the results. It is recommended to use search patterns that are as specific
as possible to the content you are looking for.

If the number of results exceeds 100, the `context` option is disabled, and 50
matches are returned without context. You can re-run the tool with a more
specific pattern or limited to specific paths to narrow down the results with
context.
fs_grep_files.examples:
Search for a function definition:
```json
{"pattern": "fn build_thread", "paths": ["crates/jp_cli/src"]}
```

Search with context lines:
```json
{"pattern": "ToolDefinition", "context": 3, "paths": ["crates/jp_llm"]}
```

Search across all files:
```json
{"pattern": "TODO|FIXME"}
```

pattern.summary: Regular expression to filter the results by.

context.summary: Number of lines of context to include before and after the matching lines.

paths.summary: Optional list of files or directories to search.
paths.description: If unspecified, all files in the project will be returned.

fs_grep_user_docs.examples:
Search for a topic:
```json
{"pattern": "tool configuration"}
```

Return entire matching files:
```json
{"pattern": "attachments", "return_entire_file": true}
```

pattern.summary: Regular expression to filter the results by.

return_entire_file.summary: Whether to return the entire file contents.
return_entire_file.description:
If enabled, the tool will return the entire file contents of any files matching
the pattern. If disabled (the default), only the matching lines and 5 contextual
lines above and below the matching lines will be returned.

fs_list_files.summary: List files in the project's local filesystem.
fs_list_files.examples:
List all Rust files:
```json
{"extensions": ["rs"]}
```

List files under a specific directory:
```json
{"prefixes": ["crates/jp_cli/src"]}
```

List all files in the project:
```json
{}
```

prefixes.summary: Optional list of path prefixes to filter the results by.
prefixes.description: If unspecified, all files in the project will be returned.

extensions.summary: Optional list of file extensions to filter the results by.
extensions.description: If unspecified, all extensions will be returned.

fs_modify_file.summary: Modify multiple files in the project's local filesystem.

path.summary: The path to the file to modify. The path must be relative to the project's root.
path.description: Default target file for all patterns. Not required if every pattern specifies its own `paths`.

patterns.summary: One or more patterns to replace in the given files. The patterns are applied in order.

old.description: The string to replace in the file. The string may span multiple lines.

new.description: The string to replace the `old` with. The string may span multiple lines. If empty, the `old` will be deleted.

paths.description: File paths to apply this pattern to. Overrides the root-level `path`. Each path must be relative to the project's root.

replace_using_regex.summary: Whether to treat `old` as a regular expression.
replace_using_regex.description:
If `true`, `old` is treated as a regular expression and the `new` is treated as a replacement string, which may contain capture groups.

If `false`, `old` is treated as a literal string and the `new` is treated as a literal string.

When `true`, replaces all non-overlapping matches in text with the replacement provided.

All instances of `$name` in the replacement string is replaced with the corresponding capture group name.

`name` may be an integer corresponding to the index of the capture group (counted by order of opening parenthesis where `0` is the entire match) or it can be a name (consisting of letters, digits or underscores) corresponding to a named capture group (e.g. `(?<name>exp)`).

If `name` isn't a valid capture group (whether the name doesn't exist or isn't a valid index), then it is replaced with the empty string.

The longest possible name is used. e.g., `$1a` looks up the capture group named `1a` and not the capture group at index `1`. To exert more precise control over the name, use braces, e.g., `${1}a`.

To write a literal `$` use `$$`.

Note that `^` matches start-of-line and `$` matches end-of-line, whereas `\A` and `\z` match the start and end of the string, respectively.

`.*` only matches single lines by default. Use `[\s\S]*` to match across lines.

fs_move_file.summary: Move a file or directory in the project's local filesystem.

source.summary: The current path of the file or directory to move. The path must be relative to the project's root.

target.summary: The new path for the file or directory. The path must be relative to the project's root.
target.description:
Parent directories will be created if they don't exist.
To rename a directory in-place, pass the directory path — do not move its files one by one.
When the source is a directory, the target must not already exist.

fs_read_file.summary: Read the contents of a file in the project's local filesystem.
fs_read_file.description:
You can use `fs_grep_files` to search for specific patterns in the file
contents, before reading the entire contents of a specific file using this tool.
fs_read_file.examples:
Read an entire file:
```json
{"path": "crates/jp_cli/src/main.rs"}
```

Read a specific line range:
```json
{"path": "crates/jp_llm/src/tool.rs", "start_line": 100, "end_line": 150}
```

path.summary: The path to the file to read.

start_line.summary: The line number to start reading from.

end_line.summary: The line number to stop reading at.

git_add_intent.summary: Mark untracked files as intent-to-add, making them visible to `git_list_patches`.
git_add_intent.description: Runs `git add --intent-to-add` for each path. This records the file in the index as an empty blob, so `git_list_patches` will show the entire file content as insertions. From there, use `git_stage_patch` or `git_stage_raw_patch` to stage all or part of the file.

paths.summary: One or more file paths to mark as intent-to-add.

git_blame.summary: Show which commits last touched each line of a file in a given range.
git_blame.description:
Returns blame information for a contiguous range of lines in a file. Output is grouped by commit: consecutive lines that share the same blamed commit are collapsed into a single hunk to keep the response compact.

Each hunk includes:

- `hash` / `short_hash`: the commit that introduced the current version of those lines.
- `previous` (when set): the commit that owned those lines *before* this change. Use this to drill back through line history — call `git_show <previous>`, `git_diff_commit <previous>`, or `git_blame --revision <previous>` to see the prior state.
- `author`, `date`, `summary`: commit metadata.
- `lines`: each line number paired with the source content.

The line range is required and capped at 200 lines per call. If you need a wider window, narrow with `pattern`-style discovery (`fs_grep_files`, `git_log -- <path>`) first, then blame the resulting range.

Defaults to the working tree (mirrors `git blame` with no revision). Pass `revision` to blame at a specific commit, tag, or branch.
git_blame.examples:
Find who last touched a function body:
```json
{"path": "crates/jp_llm/src/stream.rs", "start_line": 42, "end_line": 58}
```

Drill back one layer: blame the lines as they were before the current change, using the `previous` hash from a prior call (porcelain's `previous` is already the prior commit — do not append `^`):
```json
{"path": "crates/jp_llm/src/stream.rs", "start_line": 42, "end_line": 58, "revision": "abc1234"}
```

Ignore whitespace-only changes when assigning blame:
```json
{"path": "src/main.rs", "start_line": 1, "end_line": 30, "ignore_whitespace": true}
```

Blame at a specific tag or branch:
```json
{"path": "src/main.rs", "start_line": 100, "end_line": 110, "revision": "v0.3.0"}
```

path.summary: Path of the file to blame, relative to the workspace root.

start_line.summary: 1-based start line of the range to blame.

end_line.summary: 1-based end line of the range to blame (inclusive). The range is capped at 200 lines per call.

revision.summary: Revision to blame at (commit hash, tag, branch, or revspec). Defaults to the working tree.

ignore_whitespace.summary: Ignore whitespace-only changes when assigning blame (passes `-w` to git blame).

git_commit.summary: Commit the staged changes to the local git repository.
git_commit.examples:
```json
{"message": "feat: add lazy loading for tool descriptions

Split tool descriptions into summary and detail fields."}
```

message.summary: The commit message to use. Can be multiline.

git_diff.summary: Show the diff of changes in the git repository, filtered by staging status.
git_diff.description:
Returns a per-file summary of changes with truncated diffs. Each file's diff is capped at 50 lines. If a file is truncated, use `git_diff_file` with that file in `paths` to see its full diff (and optionally a `pattern` to search within it).

The `paths` parameter on this tool narrows which files appear in the overview but does NOT lift the per-file truncation cap.
git_diff.examples:
Diff all unstaged changes:
```json
{"status": "unstaged"}
```

Narrow the overview to specific paths:
```json
{"status": "unstaged", "paths": ["crates/jp_config/src/conversation/tool.rs"]}
```

Diff staged changes:
```json
{"status": "staged", "paths": ["src/"]}
```

paths.summary: Optional list of paths to restrict the overview to. If omitted, diffs the entire repository. Does NOT bypass per-file truncation — use `git_diff_file` for full per-file diffs.

status.summary: Which changes to include in the diff.
status.description:
- `"staged"`: changes in the index that differ from HEAD (HEAD vs index).
- `"unstaged"`: changes in the working tree that differ from the index (index vs working tree).

A staged change will NOT appear in the `"unstaged"` diff and vice versa.

git_diff_commit.summary: Show the actual diff for specific files in a commit.
git_diff_commit.description:
Returns the diff content for the given files in a commit. The `paths` parameter is required to prevent accidentally dumping massive diffs.

If the diff exceeds 500 lines, it is truncated with a message suggesting the use of the `pattern`, `start_line`, and `end_line` parameters.

When `pattern` is provided, the tool greps within the diff output and returns only matching lines with surrounding context, similar to `fs_grep_files`. This is useful for large diffs where you only care about specific changes.

When `start_line` and/or `end_line` are provided, the tool returns the given 1-based line range of the rendered diff output (mirroring `fs_read_file`). This is the escape hatch for the 500-line cap: page through a large diff in chunks rather than asking for everything at once.

`pattern` and the line range compose: when both are set, the tool greps within the requested line window — matches and context are restricted to the window, while hunk-header line numbers stay accurate to the original diff.
git_diff_commit.examples:
Show the diff for a file in a commit:
```json
{"revision": "abc1234", "paths": ["crates/jp_llm/src/stream.rs"]}
```

Show the diff for multiple files:
```json
{"revision": "HEAD~1", "paths": ["src/main.rs", "src/lib.rs"]}
```

Search within a diff:
```json
{"revision": "abc1234", "paths": ["crates/jp_config/src/lib.rs"], "pattern": "fn parse", "context": 5}
```

Page through a large commit diff (lines 500-999):
```json
{"revision": "abc1234", "paths": ["crates/jp_llm/src/stream.rs"], "start_line": 500, "end_line": 999}
```

Grep within a window of a large commit diff:
```json
{"revision": "HEAD", "paths": ["src/main.rs"], "start_line": 500, "end_line": 1500, "pattern": "TODO"}
```

revision.summary: The commit hash or any git revision specifier.

paths.summary: Files to show the diff for. Required to prevent accidental full-commit diff dumps.

pattern.summary: Regex pattern to search within the diff output. When set, only matching lines with context are returned instead of the full diff.

context.summary: Number of context lines around pattern matches. Defaults to 3 when `pattern` is set. Ignored without `pattern`.

start_line.summary: 1-based start line of the rendered diff output. When set, bypasses the 500-line truncation cap. Use this with `end_line` to page through large diffs.

end_line.summary: 1-based end line of the rendered diff output (inclusive). When set, bypasses the 500-line truncation cap. Use with `start_line` to define a window.

git_diff_file.summary: Show the full diff for specific staged or unstaged files.
git_diff_file.description:
Working-tree analog of `git_diff_commit`. Use this when `git_diff` truncates a file you want to inspect in full.

The `paths` parameter is required to prevent accidentally dumping massive diffs.

If the diff exceeds 500 lines, it is truncated with a message suggesting the use of the `pattern`, `start_line`, and `end_line` parameters.

When `pattern` is provided, the tool greps within the diff output and returns only matching lines with surrounding context, similar to `fs_grep_files`. This is useful for large diffs where you only care about specific changes.

When `start_line` and/or `end_line` are provided, the tool returns the given 1-based line range of the rendered diff output (mirroring `fs_read_file`). This is the escape hatch for the 500-line cap: page through a large diff in chunks rather than asking for everything at once.

`pattern` and the line range compose: when both are set, the tool greps within the requested line window — matches and context are restricted to the window, while hunk-header line numbers stay accurate to the original diff.
git_diff_file.examples:
Show the full unstaged diff for one file:
```json
{"status": "unstaged", "paths": ["crates/jp_llm/src/stream.rs"]}
```

Show the full staged diff for multiple files:
```json
{"status": "staged", "paths": ["src/main.rs", "src/lib.rs"]}
```

Search within a large unstaged diff:
```json
{"status": "unstaged", "paths": ["crates/jp_config/src/lib.rs"], "pattern": "fn parse", "context": 5}
```

Page through a large diff (lines 500-999):
```json
{"status": "unstaged", "paths": ["crates/jp_llm/src/stream.rs"], "start_line": 500, "end_line": 999}
```

Grep within a window of a large diff:
```json
{"status": "staged", "paths": ["src/main.rs"], "start_line": 500, "end_line": 1500, "pattern": "TODO"}
```

status.summary: Which changes to include in the diff.
status.description:
- `"staged"`: changes in the index that differ from HEAD (HEAD vs index).
- `"unstaged"`: changes in the working tree that differ from the index (index vs working tree).

A staged change will NOT appear in the `"unstaged"` diff and vice versa.

paths.summary: Files to show the diff for. Required to prevent accidental full-tree diff dumps.

pattern.summary: Regex pattern to search within the diff output. When set, only matching lines with context are returned instead of the full diff.

context.summary: Number of context lines around pattern matches. Defaults to 3 when `pattern` is set. Ignored without `pattern`.

start_line.summary: 1-based start line of the rendered diff output. When set, bypasses the 500-line truncation cap. Use this with `end_line` to page through large diffs.

end_line.summary: 1-based end line of the rendered diff output (inclusive). When set, bypasses the 500-line truncation cap. Use with `start_line` to define a window.

git_list_patches.summary: List all unstaged patches that can be staged with `git_stage_patch`.
git_list_patches.description:
Each patch has a content-addressed identifier per file (a short hex hash of the hunk text). The ID is stable across index mutations: staging one hunk does not renumber the others. Pass IDs back to `git_stage_patch` or `git_stage_patch_lines` exactly as listed.

Each diff line is prefixed with `[N]` for use with `git_stage_patch_lines`.

If the working tree changes between listing and staging (or a hunk is already staged), its ID disappears from the next listing rather than shifting position. Stale IDs passed to staging tools fail loudly instead of silently aliasing to a different hunk.
git_list_patches.examples:
List all unstaged patches across the entire repository:
```json
{}
```

List patches for specific files:
```json
{"files": ["crates/jp_config/src/conversation/tool.rs", "crates/jp_llm/src/builtin.rs"]}
```

files.summary: Optional list of files to list patches for. If omitted, lists patches for all files with unstaged changes.

git_log.description: Returns a list of commits matching the given filters. Each entry includes the short hash, author, date, and subject line. Use `git_show` to inspect a specific commit in detail.
git_log.examples:
List recent commits:
```json
{}
```

Search for commits mentioning a topic:
```json
{"query": "streaming", "count": 10}
```

Commits touching a specific file:
```json
{"paths": ["crates/jp_llm/src/stream.rs"]}
```

Commits from the last week:
```json
{"since": "1 week ago"}
```

Combine filters:
```json
{"query": "refactor", "paths": ["crates/jp_config/"], "count": 5}
```

query.summary: Search string matched against commit messages (substring match).

paths.summary: Restrict to commits touching these file paths.

count.summary: Maximum number of commits to return. Defaults to 20, capped at 50.

since.summary: Only return commits after this date. Accepts relative dates like `"2 weeks ago"` or absolute dates like `"2024-01-01"`.

git_show.summary: Show commit details: message and changed file stats (no diff content).
git_show.description: Shows the full commit message and a list of changed files with their insertion/deletion counts. Does NOT include the actual diff. Use `git_diff_commit` to view the diff for specific files.
git_show.examples:
Show a commit by hash:
```json
{"revision": "abc1234"}
```

Show the previous commit:
```json
{"revision": "HEAD~1"}
```

revision.summary: The commit hash or any git revision specifier (e.g. `HEAD~1`, a tag name, a branch name).

git_stage_patch.summary: Apply one or more patches (given their unique identifier) to the git repository index.
git_stage_patch.description:
Stages patches for one or more files in a single call. Each entry specifies a file path and the patch IDs to stage. Patch IDs come from `git_list_patches` and are content-addressed hex strings.

Batch all hunks for a single file into one call when possible — it is both faster and avoids the lock-contention retry path. Avoid issuing parallel `git_stage_patch` calls for the same file.

If an ID is no longer present in the current `git diff-files` output (because the hunk was already staged or the working tree changed), the call fails with a message instructing you to re-run `git_list_patches`.
git_stage_patch.examples:
Stage patches for a single file:
```json
{"patches": [{"path": "src/lib.rs", "ids": ["a3f9c2b18d04", "7c4e1209ab55"]}]}
```

Stage patches across multiple files:
```json
{"patches": [{"path": "src/lib.rs", "ids": ["a3f9c2b18d04"]}, {"path": "src/main.rs", "ids": ["de2f7841aa10", "61c08bb392ef"]}]}
```

patches.summary: One or more files with patch IDs to stage.

path.summary: The file path to stage patches for.

git_stage_patch_lines.summary: Stage individual lines from a patch, enabling surgical staging of partial hunks.
git_stage_patch_lines.examples:
Stage specific lines from a patch:
```json
{"path": "src/lib.rs", "patch_id": "a3f9c2b18d04", "lines": [0, 2]}
```

Stage a large contiguous range of lines:
```json
{"path": "src/lib.rs", "patch_id": "a3f9c2b18d04", "lines": ["0:50"]}
```

Mix individual indices and ranges:
```json
{"path": "src/lib.rs", "patch_id": "a3f9c2b18d04", "lines": [0, "2:10", 15]}
```

path.summary: The file path containing the patch.

lines.summary: Indices (N or "N:M") of diff lines to stage.
lines.description:
Line indices correspond to the `[N]` prefixes shown in `git_list_patches` output. Only lines prefixed with `-` or `+` have indices; context lines (prefixed with a space) do not.

Each element can be an integer (e.g. `0`) or an inclusive range string (e.g. `"1:50"` expands to lines 1 through 50). You can mix both freely.

git_unstage.summary: Unstage (restore/reset) one or more staged files, undoing the effects of `git_stage_patch`.

paths.summary: List of paths to unstage. Can be a single path or a list of paths.

github_code_search.summary: Find code matching a query in any GitHub repository.
github_code_search.description:
This tool returns a list of matched files, to fetch the actual code of a
file, use the `github_read_file` tool.

NOTE: Code search always searches the repository's default branch (usually
`main`), it is not possible to search in a specific branch or tag.
github_code_search.examples:
Search for a symbol in the current repo:
```json
{"query": "symbol:BuiltinRegistry language:rust"}
```

Search in another repository:
```json
{"query": "json in:file filename:package.json NOT path:tests/", "repository": "denoland/deno"}
```

repository.description: If unspecified, it defaults to the current project's GitHub repository.

query.description:
GitHub code-search supports:

- Bare terms (`http-push`) and quoted phrases (`"sparse index"`)
- Boolean ops: implicit AND, `OR`, `NOT`, parentheses
- Qualifiers: `language:rust`, `path:/src/**/*.rs`, `symbol:MyFunc`,
  `in:file`, `in:path`, `filename:Cargo.toml`, `extension:rs`,
  `size:>10k`
- Regex – wrap in `/.../` (`/^impl.*Display/`); escape quotes/backslashes (`\"`, `\\`)
- You must include at least one term when using qualifiers (e.g. `helper language:go`)
- You can't use the following wildcard characters as part of your
  search query: . , : ; / \ ` ' " = * ! ? # $ & + ^ | ~ < > ( ) { } [
  ] @. The search will simply ignore these symbols.

Example: `json in:file filename:package.json NOT path:tests/

github_create_issue_bug.summary: Track a new bug in the project's GitHub repository.
github_create_issue_bug.description:
- MOST IMPORTANTLY: Avoid fluff, and focus on the issue at hand. Do not add more
  text than is necessary to explain the issue.

- Use markdown to format text.

- Explain the motivation for creating the issue. You can include a comparison of
  the current behavior with the expected behavior in order to illustrate the
  impact of the issue.

- Use the body to explain what and why vs. how.

- Wrap the body at 72 characters.

- Use backticks (``) to format code or crate references.

- Add optional references to related issues or PRs in the body.

- Link to relevant code, documentation, or other resources in the body, using
  proper Github links.

- Use a narrative style to describe the issue in one or more paragraphs, avoid
  using lists, unless they are necessary to convey details about the issue.
github_create_issue_bug.examples:
```json
{
  "title": "`ToolCoordinator` panics on empty tool call list",
  "description": "When the LLM returns an empty tool call list, the coordinator panics at an unwrap.",
  "expected_behavior": "The coordinator should handle empty tool call lists gracefully.",
  "actual_behavior": "Panic at coordinator.rs:245 with 'called unwrap on None'.",
  "complexity": "low"
}
```

title.summary: The title of the bug to track.
title.description:
Should be a single line, not include any markdown except for backticks (`) where
applicable. Keep the title short and descriptive.

description.summary: A clear and concise description of what the issue is about.

expected_behavior.summary: A description of the expected behavior.

actual_behavior.summary: A description of the actual behavior.

complexity.description: This is used to estimate the effort required to fix the issue.

reproduce.summary: Optional notes on how to reproduce the issue.
reproduce.description:
Only needed if `description`, `expected_behavior`, and `actual_behavior`
are not sufficient to explain the issue.

proposed_solution.summary: Optional proposed solution to the issue.
proposed_solution.description:
Should be a high-level, SHORT description. Keep it brief, avoid detail.
If code is added, use pseudo-code to avoid obsolescence.

tasks.summary: Optional ordered tasks to resolve the bug.
tasks.description: Include links to specific lines of code where the task should happen at.

resource_links.summary: Optional list of resource links relevant to the issue.

labels.summary: Additional labels to add to the issue.
labels.description:
- The issue will always be assigned the `bug` label.
- If unspecified, no additional labels will be added.
- Only labels that exist on the project can be added, non-existing labels will
  result in an error with a list of valid labels, so you can retry again.

assignees.summary: Assignees to add to the issue.
assignees.description:
- You should only add assignees if explicitly requested by the user.
- If unspecified, no assignees will be added.
- Only collaborators on the project can be added, non-existing assignees will
  result in an error with a list of valid assignees, so you can retry again.

github_create_issue_enhancement.summary: File a new enhancement request in the project's GitHub repository.
github_create_issue_enhancement.description:
- MOST IMPORTANTLY: Avoid fluff, and focus on the enhancement at hand. Do not
  add more text than is necessary to explain the enhancement.

- Use markdown to format text.

- Explain the motivation for creating the issue. You can include a comparison of
  the current behavior with the expected behavior in order to illustrate the
  impact of the request.

- Use the body to explain what and why vs. how.

- Wrap the body at 72 characters.

- Use backticks (``) to format code or crate references.

- Add optional references to related issues or PRs in the body.

- Link to relevant code, documentation, or other resources in the body, using
  proper Github links.

- Use a narrative style to describe the issue in one or more paragraphs, avoid
  using lists, unless they are necessary to convey details about the issue.
github_create_issue_enhancement.examples:
```json
{
  "title": "Support lazy loading of tool descriptions",
  "description": "Tool descriptions consume significant context. Allow LLMs to load details on demand.",
  "context": "Large tool descriptions eat into the context window, reducing space for conversation history.",
  "complexity": "medium"
}
```

title.summary: The title of the enhancement to track.
title.description:
Should be a single line, not include any markdown except for backticks (`) where
applicable. Keep the title short and descriptive.

description.summary: A clear and concise description of what the enhancement request is about.
description.description: Explain the motivation for creating the issue.

context.summary: What are you trying to do and how would you want it to be different?
context.description: Is it something you currently cannot do? Is this related to an issue/problem?

complexity.description: This is used to estimate the effort required to implement the enhancement.

alternatives.summary: Can the same result be achieved in an alternative way?

proposed_implementation.summary: Optional proposed implementation for the enhancement.
proposed_implementation.description:
Should be a high-level, SHORT description. Keep it brief, avoid detail.
If code is added, use pseudo-code to avoid obsolescence.

tasks.summary: Optional ordered tasks to implement the enhancement.
tasks.description: Include links to specific lines of code where the task should happen at.

resource_links.summary: Optional list of resource links relevant to the issue.

labels.summary: Additional labels to add to the issue.
labels.description:
- The issue will always be assigned the `enhancement` label.
- If unspecified, no additional labels will be added.
- Only labels that exist on the project can be added, non-existing labels will
  result in an error with a list of valid labels, so you can retry again.

assignees.summary: Assignees to add to the issue.
assignees.description:
- You should only add assignees if explicitly requested by the user.
- If unspecified, no assignees will be added.
- Only collaborators on the project can be added, non-existing assignees will
  result in an error with a list of valid assignees, so you can retry again.

github_create_issue_rfd_tracking.summary: Create a tracking issue for an RFD in the project's GitHub repository.
github_create_issue_rfd_tracking.description:
Creates a GitHub issue to track the implementation of an RFD. The issue title, label, and body structure are fixed — you only provide the RFD number, title, filename slug, and task list.

Read the attached RFD carefully. Extract the number, title, and slug from the filename. Derive tasks from the Implementation Plan section — use phase names, step names, or a concise summary of each deliverable.
github_create_issue_rfd_tracking.examples:
```json
{
  "rfd_number": "042",
  "rfd_title": "Conversation Compaction",
  "rfd_slug": "042-conversation-compaction",
  "tasks": [
    "Phase 1: Mechanical compaction strategies",
    "Phase 2: LLM-assisted summarization",
    "Phase 3: Integration with conversation storage"
  ]
}
```

rfd_slug.summary: The RFD filename without the .md extension (e.g. '042-conversation-compaction').

tasks.summary: Task list derived from the RFD's Implementation Plan.
tasks.description: Each task becomes a checkbox in the issue body. Derive tasks from the Implementation Plan section of the RFD.

github_issues.summary: Find one or more issues in a GitHub repository.
github_issues.examples:
List open issues in the current project's repo:
```json
{"state": "open"}
```

List every issue (open and closed) by leaving `state` unset:
```json
{}
```

Get details for a specific issue, including the first page of comments:
```json
{"number": 42}
```

Fetch an issue from a different repository:
```json
{"repository": "Swatinem/rust-cache", "number": 37}
```

Walk further into a long comment thread:
```json
{"repository": "Swatinem/rust-cache", "number": 37, "page": 2}
```

repository.summary: Repository to read issues from, in `owner/repo` form.
repository.description: If unspecified, defaults to the current project's GitHub repository.

number.summary: Issue number to get information about.
number.description:
If unspecified, a single page of issues will be returned, without the
issue contents or comments. Re-run the tool with the issue number to
fetch its details and comments.

state.description: If unspecified, both open and closed issues will be returned.

page.description:
When `number` is set, selects a page of conversation comments (10 per
page); the response includes `comments_count` (total) so you can tell
when the thread is exhausted.

When `number` is unset, selects a page of issues (100 per page). The
response does not include a grand total — request the next page and
stop when fewer than 100 issues come back.

github_list_files.summary: List all files in a GitHub repository.
github_list_files.description: Can be combined with `github_read_file` to fetch the contents of a file.
github_list_files.examples:
List all files in the current repo:
```json
{}
```

List files in a subdirectory of another repo:
```json
{"repository": "owner/repo", "path": "src/"}
```

github_read_file.summary: Fetch the contents of one or more files
github_read_file.examples:
Read a file from the default branch:
```json
{"path": "Cargo.toml"}
```

Read from a specific branch in another repository:
```json
{"path": "src/main.rs", "ref": "develop", "repository": "owner/repo"}
```

repository.description: If unspecified, it defaults to the current project's GitHub repository.

ref.description: If unspecified, it defaults to the repository's default branch (usually `main`)

path.summary: File path to get contents of.
path.description: If unspecified, it defaults to the root of the repository.

start_line.summary: 1-based line number to start reading from.
start_line.description:
Reads from this line through `end_line` (or the end of the file). If unset,
reads from line 1.

end_line.summary: 1-based line number to stop reading at (inclusive).
end_line.description:
If unset, the tool reads through the end of the file when the file is small
enough. To keep huge generated files out of the LLM context, files larger
than 2,000 lines are truncated to 2,000 lines starting from `start_line`
(or line 1 when also unset) when `end_line` is missing. The response
appends a marker like `(truncated to lines X-Y of N; pass start_line and
end_line to read a different range)` so you can re-call to read further.

With an explicit `end_line`, no implicit cap is applied — you get exactly
the range you asked for.

github_pr_diff.summary: Inspect the files changed in a pull request.
github_pr_diff.examples:
Enumerate the changed files in a PR (filenames + stats, no patches):
```json
{"number": 42}
```

Fetch the patches for two specific files in the PR. The filenames must
match what `enumerate` mode returns:
```json
{"number": 42, "files": ["crates/jp_llm/src/builtin.rs", "Cargo.toml"]}
```

Inspect a PR from a different repository:
```json
{"repository": "Swatinem/rust-cache", "number": 37}
```

Walk further into a PR with more than 100 changed files (rare):
```json
{"number": 42, "page": 2}
```

repository.summary: Repository to read the pull request from, in `owner/repo` form.
repository.description: If unspecified, defaults to the current project's GitHub repository.

number.description:
Required. Use `github_pulls` (without `number`) first if you need to
discover available PR numbers.

files.summary: List of changed file paths to fetch patches for.
files.description:
If unspecified, the tool returns a page of changed file metadata
(filename, status, additions, deletions, changes) without patches, plus
the total `changed_files_count` for the PR. Use this to decide which
files to fetch.

If set, the tool returns the patches for the named files. Filenames not
present on the current `page` are returned in a `not_found` block; bump
`page` or call without `files` to locate them.

page.summary: 1-indexed page of changed files. Defaults to 1.
page.description:
Selects a page of changed files (100 per page). For PRs with more than
100 changed files, walk pages until the page is short. The response
exposes `changed_files_count` so you can tell when the list is
exhausted.

`files` and `page` work together: when `files` is set, the tool searches
that page for the requested filenames and returns their patches plus a
`not_found` block for any that aren't on this page.

github_pr_review_add_comment.summary: Append one inline comment to your draft (PENDING) review on a pull request.
github_pr_review_add_comment.description:
Adds a single inline review comment to the PR's pending review by the
authenticated user (token from `JP_GITHUB_TOKEN` or `GITHUB_TOKEN`). If no
pending review exists yet, an empty one is created on the first call. The
review is left in `PENDING` state and is visible only to the author until
they explicitly submit it via the GitHub UI.

Each call queues exactly one comment. To add several comments, call this
tool repeatedly. The order of calls matches the order the comments appear
in the GitHub UI.

Anchoring rules:
- `path`: the file's path in the PR (relative to the repo root).
- `line`: 1-based line number. For a single-line comment, this is the line
  the comment is anchored to. For a multi-line comment, this is the LAST
  line of the range.
- `side`: `RIGHT` (default) anchors to the new file (the lines you'd see
  with `+` or as context in the diff). `LEFT` anchors to the old file
  (removed `-` lines, using their old-file line numbers).
- `start_line` (optional): first line of a multi-line range. Must be `<=
  line`.
- `start_side` (optional): side for the start line; defaults to `side`.

The `body` is rendered as GitHub-flavored Markdown.
github_pr_review_add_comment.examples:
A single-line comment on the new file:
```json
{
  "pull_number": 42,
  "path": "crates/jp_cli/src/cmd/query.rs",
  "line": 128,
  "body": "This `unwrap` is reachable when the channel closes early."
}
```

A multi-line comment spanning lines 200-215:
```json
{
  "pull_number": 42,
  "path": "crates/jp_llm/src/provider.rs",
  "start_line": 200,
  "line": 215,
  "side": "RIGHT",
  "body": "Consider extracting this loop into a helper; it's hard to follow."
}
```

A comment on a removed line:
```json
{
  "pull_number": 42,
  "path": "crates/jp_cli/src/lib.rs",
  "line": 42,
  "side": "LEFT",
  "body": "Why was this removed? It looks load-bearing."
}
```

pull_number.summary: The pull request number to comment on.

path.summary: Path of the file in the PR, relative to the repository root.

line.summary: 1-based line number for the comment's anchor.
line.description:
For a single-line comment, this is the line the comment is anchored to.
For a multi-line comment, this is the LAST line of the range; pair it with
`start_line` for the first.

body.summary: Comment text. Rendered as GitHub-flavored Markdown.

side.summary: Diff side. RIGHT = new file (default), LEFT = old file.

start_line.summary: First line of a multi-line comment range. Must be <= `line`.

github_pr_review_add_reply.summary: Append one reply to an existing inline review comment thread on a pull request.
github_pr_review_add_reply.description:
Adds a reply to an existing review-comment thread on the PR. The reply is
attached to the authenticated user's pending (PENDING) review via GraphQL
`addPullRequestReviewThreadReply`, so it stays a draft alongside any
queued top-level comments. If no pending review exists yet, an empty one
is created on the first call. The review remains in `PENDING` state until
the user submits it via the GitHub UI.

Use this when triaging existing review comments and you want to record a
verdict, ask a clarifying question, or note how a comment was addressed —
without making the reply visible to other readers of the PR yet.

Anchoring rules:
- `comment_id`: the GitHub REST integer ID of the comment you are
  replying to. The `gh:pull/N/reviews` attachment surfaces this id next
  to each comment as `id=<n>`. Pass the id of the specific comment in
  the thread you want to reply to; GitHub flattens replies into the
  thread regardless of which comment in the thread you target.
- `body`: rendered as GitHub-flavored Markdown.

Each call queues exactly one reply. To reply to several threads, call
this tool repeatedly. The order of calls matches the order the replies
appear in the GitHub UI within their respective threads.
github_pr_review_add_reply.examples:
A short reply confirming a verdict:
```json
{
  "pull_number": 42,
  "comment_id": 2371234567,
  "body": "Good catch — switching to a guard struct in the next push."
}
```

A reply pushing back on a review comment:
```json
{
  "pull_number": 42,
  "comment_id": 2371234567,
  "body": "I disagree: this `unwrap` is reachable only after `init`, which is enforced by the type system. See `Builder::build` for the invariant."
}
```

pull_number.summary: The pull request number containing the thread.

comment_id.summary: GitHub REST integer ID of the comment to reply to.
comment_id.description:
Surfaced in the `gh:pull/N/reviews` attachment as `id=<n>` on each comment
line. GitHub flattens replies into the thread, so any comment id from the
target thread works — pick the one you're directly responding to.

body.summary: Reply text. Rendered as GitHub-flavored Markdown.

github_pulls.summary: Find one or more pull requests in a GitHub repository.
github_pulls.examples:
List open pull requests in the current project's repo:
```json
{"state": "open"}
```

List every PR (open and closed) by leaving `state` unset:
```json
{}
```

Get details for a specific PR, including the first page of conversation
comments:
```json
{"number": 42}
```

Fetch a PR from a different repository:
```json
{"repository": "Swatinem/rust-cache", "number": 37}
```

Walk further into a long comment thread:
```json
{"repository": "Swatinem/rust-cache", "number": 37, "page": 2}
```

To inspect a PR's actual code changes, use the separate `github_pr_diff`
tool — `github_pulls` only returns metadata and conversation.

repository.summary: Repository to read pull requests from, in `owner/repo` form.
repository.description: If unspecified, defaults to the current project's GitHub repository.

number.summary: Pull request number to get information about.
number.description:
If unspecified, a single page of pull requests will be returned, without
the PR descriptions or comments. Re-run the tool with the PR number to
fetch its details and conversation comments.

state.summary: Filter pull requests by their state.
state.description: If unspecified, both open and closed pull requests will be returned.

page.description:
When `number` is set, selects a page of conversation comments (10 per
page); the response includes `comments_count` (total) so you can tell
when the thread is exhausted. Inline review comments are not returned
by this tool.

When `number` is unset, selects a page of pull requests (100 per page).
The response does not include a grand total — request the next page and
stop when fewer than 100 pull requests come back.

The response in `number` mode also exposes `changed_files_count`; use
`github_pr_diff` to fetch the actual file changes.

nnn.description: ID of the RFD to abandon.

rfd_extend.description: Record that RFD MMM extends RFD NNN, updating both documents.

nnn.description: ID of the RFD being extended (the older, established RFD). Must be in Accepted or Implemented status.

mmm.description: ID of the RFD that extends the other (the newer RFD).

rfd_promote.description:
Promote an RFD to the next stage (Draft -> Discussion -> Accepted -> Implemented).

For drafts (DNN-prefixed), assigns a permanent number and moves the file from `docs/rfd/drafts/` up to `docs/rfd/`. When promoting to Accepted, creates a GitHub tracking issue via `jp`.

nnn.description: ID of the RFD to promote. Accepts a permanent number (41, 041) or a draft ID (D01).

rfd_require.description: Record that RFD NNN requires RFD MMM, updating both documents. Cycles are refused. The relationship is gated identically to `rfd_extend`: an Accepted RFD may not depend on something below Accepted, and an Implemented RFD may not depend on something below Implemented. Don't list the same target under both `Requires` and `Extends` — extension implies the dependency. Accepts draft IDs (DNN) on either side, subject to the draft-aware bidirectional rule (a published RFD cannot link to a draft).

nnn.description: ID of the RFD that depends on the other (the requirer). Permanent number (e.g. 043) or draft ID (e.g. D07).

mmm.description: ID of the RFD that is required (the dependency). Permanent number (e.g. 012) or draft ID (e.g. D05).

nnn.description: ID of the RFD to supersede.

rust_crate_readme.summary: Get a crate's README as markdown.
rust_crate_readme.description: Fetches the README for a specific crate version and converts it from HTML to markdown. Useful for a quick overview of a crate's purpose, feature flags, and typical usage before diving into the item docs.

crate_version.summary: Semver-compatible version, or `latest` for the most recent release.

rust_crate_resource.summary: Fetch a crate resource (metadata, README, items, source) by URI.
rust_crate_resource.description:
Single entry point for everything else bookworm exposes. Supported URI shapes:

- `crate://{name}` — list non-yanked crate versions
- `crate://{name}/{version}` — get crate metadata
- `crate://{name}/{version}/readme` — README as markdown
- `crate://{name}/{version}/items` — list all item resources
- `crate://{name}/{version}/src` — list source file resources
- `crate://{name}/{version}/items/{path}` — fetch a specific item's docs
- `crate://{name}/{version}/src/{path}` — fetch a specific source file

`{version}` may be a concrete semver (`1.0.140`), a partial semver (`1`), or `latest` for the most recent release.
rust_crate_resource.examples:
List versions of serde_json:
```json
{"uri": "crate://serde_json"}
```

Fetch the docs for a specific method:
```json
{"uri": "crate://serde_json/1.0.140/items/value/enum.Value.html#method.pointer"}
```

Read a source file:
```json
{"uri": "crate://tokio/latest/src/tokio/runtime/mod.rs.html"}
```

rust_crate_search_items.summary: Search for item definitions within a crate.
rust_crate_search_items.description:
Returns a list of items (modules, types, functions, methods, traits, variants, etc.) inside the given crate that match a partial-path query, each with its canonical path, kind, signature, prose docs (as markdown), and resource URIs for drilling in further.

The query is case-insensitive partial-match against both the item's name and its full path. Use `kinds` to narrow the results — e.g. only methods, only traits — when you know what you're after.
rust_crate_search_items.examples:
Find any `Value` symbol in serde_json:
```json
{"crate_name": "serde_json", "query": "Value"}
```

Find methods on `Value`:
```json
{"crate_name": "serde_json", "query": "Value::", "kinds": ["Method"]}
```

Narrow by version:
```json
{"crate_name": "tokio", "crate_version": "1.40", "query": "spawn"}
```

crate_version.summary: Semver-compatible version, or `latest` for the most recent release.

query.summary: Partial-match query against item names and paths.

kinds.summary: Filter by item kind. Allowed values: Constant, Derive, Enum, Function, Macro, Method, Module, Struct, Trait, Type, Variant, Attribute. Omit for all kinds.

rust_crate_versions.summary: List recent versions of a crate.
rust_crate_versions.description: Returns the most recent non-yanked versions of the named crate, along with each version's release date, download count, MSRV, and publisher. Useful for picking a concrete version to feed into `rust_crate_search_items` or `rust_crate_resource`.

rust_crates_search.summary: Search crates.io for crates matching a query.
rust_crates_search.description:
Returns a list of crates from crates.io that match the query, along with each crate's name, latest version, description, download count, and homepage / documentation / repository links. Each result is keyed by a `crate://` URI that can be passed back to `rust_crate_resource` to drill in further.

Prefer broad keyword queries over exact phrases — the underlying search is fuzzy and works best with a few descriptive terms.
rust_crates_search.examples:
Find an HTTP client crate:
```json
{"query": "http client"}
```

Find a JSON parser:
```json
{"query": "serde json"}
```

unix_utils.description:
A collection of useful unix tools available for quick tasks. Each tool is
invoked as a subprocess with the given arguments. Output is returned as-is
(truncated at 100 KB).

Path arguments are sandboxed to the workspace root — absolute paths and
`..` traversals that escape the workspace are rejected.

See the `util` parameter enum values for the list of available tools.
unix_utils.examples:
Get the current date:
```json
{"util": "date"}
```

Format a date:
```json
{"util": "date", "args": ["+%Y-%m-%d"]}
```

Base64 encode a string:
```json
{"util": "base64", "stdin": "Hello, World!"}
```

Base64 decode:
```json
{"util": "base64", "args": ["--decode"], "stdin": "SGVsbG8sIFdvcmxkIQ=="}
```

Calculate an expression:
```json
{"util": "bc", "stdin": "2^32"}
```

Generate a UUID:
```json
{"util": "uuidgen"}
```

Count lines in a file:
```json
{"util": "wc", "args": ["-l", "src/main.rs"]}
```

Compute a SHA-256 checksum:
```json
{"util": "shasum", "args": ["-a", "256", "Cargo.lock"]}
```

Detect file type:
```json
{"util": "file", "args": "Cargo.lock"}
```

First 20 lines of a file:
```json
{"util": "head", "args": ["-n", "20", "README.md"]}
```

Last 10 lines of a log:
```json
{"util": "tail", "args": ["-n", "10", "output.log"]}
```

Extract a field from JSON:
```json
{"util": "jq", "args": ".version", "stdin": "{"name":"jp","version":"0.1.0"}"}
```

Sort lines:
```json
{"util": "sort", "stdin": "banana\napple\ncherry"}
```

Deduplicate sorted lines:
```json
{"util": "sort", "args": "-u", "stdin": "a\nb\na\nb\nc"}
```

Print OS and architecture:
```json
{"util": "uname", "args": "-sm"}
```

args.summary: Arguments to pass to the utility.

stdin.summary: Optional string to pipe to the utility's standard input.

web_fetch.summary: Fetch the contents of a web page over HTTP(S).

url.summary: The URL of the web page to fetch.

list_sections.summary: return a compact listing of all anchored sections on the page (headings and definition-list terms)
list_sections.description: Each entry includes the section's anchor ID, level, title, and a short content preview. Use the anchor IDs with the `sections` parameter to fetch specific sections. Works for both HTML and markdown sources; HTML also surfaces AsciiDoctor-style `<dt>` glossary terms (e.g. git's manpages).

sections.summary: List of section anchor IDs to fetch.
sections.description: Only the content under those anchors will be returned, significantly reducing output size. Use `list_sections` first to discover available IDs. For headings, IDs are consistent across markdown and HTML fetches (GitHub-style heading slugs); for HTML definition-list terms the ID is whatever the page exposes (typically a permalink anchor).

describe_tools.description: Get detailed descriptions and usage examples for one or more tools.

tools.description: Array of tool names to describe
